content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Mean Girls (2004) is still sooo good

**Score:** 63
**Body:** Just rewatched mean girls and I am still a huge fan of it and it still seems so much fun to watch.

It is one of those movies I can never get bored of rewatching, it does perfectly sum up the high school life and drama.

Also, I am looking for similar movie recommendations, I have tried watching a few but nothing really compares.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 1. Superbad

2. The edge of seventeen 

3. Easy A

4. Ferris Bueller’s Day Off

5. Clueless
  - These and add Dazed and Confused
  - These and:

Donnie Darko
Disturbia
The Stepfather
30 Minutes or Less
Project X
Scary Movie 1 & 2 
2. Heathers!
  - So good, and definitely laid the groundwork for Mean Girls.


Just avoid the TV show remake. It went "but what if the gays and the woke were the villains?", which kinda missed the whole point and made the show quite bad.
  - Mean Girls is just Heathers without any cajones. 

Heathers is an 18 rated movie that discusses several taboo issues that would get uploads instantly banned from youtube, and in a funny way that is believable from high school students,

Mean Girls is a PG movie that has teens saying some hurty words to each other but they all make friends at the end. 
3. Jawbreaker 

Heathers
4. Juno (more of a dramedy though)

Napoleon Dynamite
5. How is this movie 22 years old?!!
6. john tucker must die!!

## 2. Magic (1978) with Anthony Hopkins

**Score:** 49
**Body:** Woah! This film completely blew my mind after watching it last night. It's fast paced, super unnerving, and morbidly chaotic. I ALMOST am willing to say it's a better portrayal of madness than Anthony Perkins in Hitchcock's Psycho. 10/10 from me to you-for anyone who digs character background and twisted storylines. Favorite movie from Hopkins BY FAR.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Written by William Goldman, of The Princess Bride and Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
2. Saw this as part of a double feature with Alien, needless to say I was a wreck after this!
3. Love this movie…it just starts right off and takes you on a ride 
4. love this one, underrated.
5. I've put it on my watch list!
6. "Traumatized" gets thrown around a lot these day

But I watched this alone after dark in our big house at around 9 years old. (pre internet and cell phones).

I was scared out of my mind.
7. Underrated movie 💯
8. I wanted to watch this when it came out but I was 10 and my smart mom said no.
9. Yeah, this was a neat little hidden gem I found fir myself in Tubi a couple years back. I was flipping through movies and I was like, "is that a young Anthony Hopkins peaking from behind that ventroliqist dummy?!?! Yes please! 


Happily, it wasnt an unintentionally funny B-movie, like I was expecting, but instead a great little thriller/drama.
10. Just the movie trailer freaked out my childhood brain and I’ve never felt entirely comfortable re: ventriloquist dummies ever since.  Also, still have no interest in seeing the movie. 

## 3. Midsommar (2019)

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Totally unexpected , one should definitely go for this one if looking for something adventurous and unexpected 

a group of friends went out on a trip and one of them betrays them all that's all I can tell you all about this 

in order to find out you should go and check it out you're going to love it if you like gore movies , it's not like any Slasher movie it's a bit horror, thriller a lot , suspense other than action and romance you're gonna see a lot of things in this one 

go and check it out 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This one might need a second watch for those people that didn’t like it. I did. Now I’ve seen it several times. 
  - Yea I didn’t care for it when it came out. But I didn’t hate it?

I love Hereditary but it felt like Aster was just kicking up the edginess with Midsommar. I’ll probably watch it again this year. Some movies just don’t click until you reach certain milestones 
2. director cut also
  - The directors cut wound up the intensity times 10
3. It was very disturbing to me
4. It was ok but I've never looked at it with the reverence it seems to get on reddit

Could have shaved plenty of time off it, too
5. Once of my favourite cinema experiences I didn’t know much about the film at all

I found the opening just jaw dropping

Some of the best psychedelic drug experience depiction in cinema 

Love the skill of making horror in the daylight 

Anyone that enjoyed Midsommar should check out the film it’s paying homage to, The Wicker Man (1973)  


6. Fantastic movie.  Florence Pugh should have won an Oscar for her performance.  My favorite horror movie.
7. we need more folky cult horror 
8. As a Swede, it was weird to watch. Midsommar is a family friendly gathering, not something sinnister. I thought it was a comedy at first.

## 4. Feel-good and uplifting Hard Sci-Fi films like Project Hail Mary

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Too much bad vibes happening in the world right now, PHM is quite a breath of fresh air. Same with The Martian. Now I'd like to watch more of the same. Any suggestions?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wouldn't call it a feel good movie exactly but Contact is  great and feels like the blueprint for the big swing sci fi films that came after it like PHM   

  - I get on reddit just to peek comments to steal movies to watch, thank you Bruce
2. This is an old one — Enemy Mine, about a human and an alien who are mortal enemies in a space war who become stranded on a desolate planet together. It’s a solid, fun ‘80s movie with a good message. 
  - This was one of my all-time favourite films when I was a kid! You've just reminded me and I'm going to watch it with my family tonight ❤️ it is a tearjerker though! 
3. It's not a movie, but you should still give it a chance: *Star Trek - The Next Generation*.

The series tackles a wide range of themes with a lot of optimism. When I think of "the best of humanity", the crew of the Enterprise comes to mind.
4. 2010: The Year We Make Contact (1984)
5. Abyss 
6. Cocoon? Fantastic Ron Howard movie from the 80s.
7. Apollo 13
8. Wall -E counts??

## 5. Can you recommend existential movies for me to watch?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Some of my favourite movies that make me reflect on life include the matrix, lotr, inception and inside out

I'm looking for movies within various genres (other than horror and musicals) which get me to question life, human nature, purpose, good vs evil

I'd prefer if the movies are no older than the mid to late 70s, and if they're not the usual titles which are recommended when the above come to mind

Thank you

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of the quintissential existentialist movies I've seen is I Heart Huckabees.
2. eXistenZ (1999)
3. Arrival (2016) 
4. Waking Life
5. Krzysztof Kieslowski’s “Three Colors” trilogy is fantastic (Red, White and Blue) and addresses all of the themes in your post in profound ways 
6. Soul, Palm Springs
7. No Country for Old Men. 
8. Solaris 
9. Arrival. I cried like a baby at the end. 
10. A.I. Artificial Intelligence

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Denis Villeneuve casually dropped a whole trilogy between 5 years.

**Score:** 7900

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Time for a remake. First one is over half a decade old. 
  - Dune the TV series brought to you by Amazon starring Shaquille O'Neal as Paul Atreides. 
    - Zendaya plays the same part 😂
      - Haha that's a better joke! 
    - Mr beast as baron harkonnen
2. This seems fairly normal for trilogies. Often you’d see two year gaps between each, so if anything the 3 year gap from ‘21 to ‘24 makes this a little longer than a normal quick turnaround trilogy.

But the quality and confidence of vision demonstrated by these films is praiseworthy regardless of the release timeline. 

3. Is that an achievement? Lotr trilogy was dropped in 3years
  - LOTR trilogy was all filmed at the same time iirc
4. Is there literally anything casual about dropping 3 major, quality blockbusters across 5 years? It doesn't really feel like there would be.
  - Right, we should kill op, I fucking hate him.

## 2. Finally watched The Green Mile, and that devastating ending really got to me. The performances were top-notch.

**Score:** 1808
**Body:** Where does The Green Mile stand for you among Stephen King adaptations?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Michael Clarke Duncan is amazing here.
  - I still cry after probably 10x seeing it
    - He really does kill that role
2. Incredible movie. Incredible. 
3. My mom always said this part of the movie stayed with her and is why she can never watch it again. All my dumb ass remembers from the movie is Tom Hanks peeing on his lawn. 
4. The Shawshank Redemption is obviously the best Stephen King adaptation but the Green Mile is also amazing 
  - I think Stand By Me deserves to be mentioned as well. It's an almost perfect movie. Personally it edges out the Green Mile by a hair, but Shawshank is probably still the best. 
5. I love saying "I's tired boss". 
6. I could only watch this the whole way once. 
7. Roll on two... 😭😭😭😭

## 3. Best comedic moment in a serious scene.

**Score:** 1805

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not really a comedic moment but I found this funny

![gif](giphy|135E47VKw6TM6A)
  - Yes. This whole scene is hilarious in a serious scene.

    - The best directors (Tarantino, Lynch, Cronenberg, Verhoeven) know how to properly mix the intense and suspenseful with the absurd and funny in a way that seems surreal while also mimicking how bizarre real life can be. And if this isn't that most pretentious sentence of my life, I don't want to know what is.
2. Falling Down (1993)

https://preview.redd.it/7xrp62k0tlug1.jpeg?width=1920&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=c65d6e2e60d959f322b34ee138703f84cb5ad2c3


3. https://preview.redd.it/44ec0ph2gmug1.jpeg?width=634&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=8e3eaf41db8e65b29b3767bc070ace0bbbb56260

I thought seeing Firefly’s Captain Reynolds as the wrong Private Ryan was pretty funny.  (Nathan Fillion)
  - *Sobbing* "So my brother's okay?"

*Walking away* "Yeah I'm sure he's fine"
4. https://preview.redd.it/sxra24xbrmug1.jpeg?width=600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=df51b452117bef434aaafc2516c6469a418b8cdb

I wouldn’t call Skull Island a serious film, but this scene still makes me laugh for all its build up as a heroic moment.
5. *Processing img ei3b3aqbqlug1...*

The Propeller Guy from Titanic.
6. Sonny: Is there any special country you want to go to?

Sal: …Wyoming

https://i.redd.it/abs2nfhbmlug1.gif
7. https://i.redd.it/kb3r8x9u6lug1.gif

## 4. What is the most bizarre, out of nowhere scene you’ve ever seen?

**Score:** 1590
**Body:** What are some movie scenes for you that are so strange and jarring in an otherwise normal movie, it lives rent free in your head?

For me it’s the dance sequence in Ex Machina. It’s so random and surreal! Oscar Isaac nails it here, he really sells the crazed scientist persona.

What’s that one scene for you?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|14c6pahskzHD2g)
  - Dont forget, the boat had the exact number of seats for the people who were on it. Meaning that the tour was not going to proceed until the appropriate children had succumbed to the appropriate vices.
2. It make sense on rewatch but yeah, first time was real WTF with that scene.

In my youth, this one was wild:

![gif](giphy|j78SiVGH0WCWmRo8KL)
3. Aim for the bushes?
4. The Zoolander gas pump dance scene was very random even for that movie
  - It was early enough to set the tone for the rest of the movie, though, so I feel like it’s more communicating that we should expect immense amounts of randomness.
  - "Just because we have chiseled abs and stunning features, it doesn't mean that we too can't not die in a freak gasoline fight accident.”
5. This dance bit to me took the movie to a higher level.
I don’t know what it is but it just flows.
  - It makes sense for the character too. He’s an anti-social alcoholic who built a sex robot. This guy getting tipsy and making the robot dance with him is one of the less surprising things he does. 
6. The psychedelic bowling scene in The Big Lebowski is a tad ‘out there’.

## 5. Are there any children of former A-listers you now prefer over their famous father?

**Score:** 559

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Gleeson and Skarsgard families are getting really hard to make that determination, with all the great work they keep putting out.

Edit: anyone looking for a great show, check out Murderbot.
  - I’m having trouble keeping all of the hyper talented Skarsgaard offspring in order. I can only talk about them like they’re endangered cranes or something.

Ex, I think a new one just reached maturity after it migrated from the Sweden breeding grounds. 
  - I liked the Gleason kid before I knew who he was. That’s how it should be. 
  - Skarsgards are fucking great actors all around. 
2. Why don't people ever talk about Meg Ryan when they talk about Jack Quaid? Just curious.
  - Maybe it’s just the last name . People naturally go there first.
3. Jack Quaid is Hollywood's Good boy, so definitely him.
4. I’d say Michael Douglas did that back in the 80s. Of the pictures shown nobody has got near what their parent(s) have done as yet. Some will say Jamie Lee Curtis but she’s in the shadow of 2 greats and Tony was one of the last huge stars of the Golden Age. One could argue for Kate Hudson over Goldie Hawn but if we also take into account her stepdad Kurt Russell then again she drops off. Bryce Dallas Howard goes clear over her dad in the acting stakes but in overall career comes out just behind. 
5. Jared Harris
6. Ben Stiller. 

My friends grew up in the acting world and they grew up around Ben. Him getting out of his fathers shadow was a big deal.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A small detail in Caché (Michael Haneke, 2005) that may reinforce one of the theories

**Score:** 45
**Body:** In Caché it is never explicitely revealed who sends the tapes to the Laurent family. One of the possible interpretations is that the director himself is recording and sending those tapes. I will put the clues for this idea at the bottom of this post, for those that have not catched them. This whole idea can mean different things, depending on who you ask. A very common way of seeing this is that Haneke is intentionally blurring the line between the stalking footage and the act of filming itself. Almost as if he wanted to make the characters aware that they are being filmed by him.

# The detail I have noticed

Around 20 minuted in, we see a [postcard](https://imgur.com/a/VCGXCDz) that Pierrot receives at school, and we can see the address of the school: II Rue Pirandello, in the XIII arrondissement of Paris, the real name of the road where the building is. 

Pirandello, the dramaturge famous for the idea of characters becoming aware of being in a work of fiction, of being observed. Isn't this entire film similar to the concept of "destruction of the paper roof", that he discusses in the novel "Il fu Mattia Pascal", or the entire play "Six characters in search of an author"?

# The other clues for this intepretation are:

* The tapes' footage has the same image characteristic of the rest of the film; in fact, we can see the shadow of a cinema-grade camera in one of the tapes' frame. I am not an expert and cannot identify the model, but I can bet that it is indeed the same Sony CineAlta HDW-F900 used for the rest of the film.
* A tape almost magically manifests in the door range of Laurents' apartment. Georges was there all the time, and only cinema magic can have put it there.
* In Majid appartment we can clearly see that there is no hidden camera when Georges goes confronting him the first time. Considering the quality of the images and the fact it was 2004 (hence the camera must have been pretty large), it was impossible to conceal such a camera so perfectly.



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought in the ending shot in front of the school we see in the background his son and the other kid interact, and it's implied his own son was involved with sending the tapes? I can't quite remember though its been years since I last saw it
  - I read that more as a hopeful thematic endnote. It's up to the youth to transgress the sins of their father (and inherited trauma and racism in a wider lens) and to forge a better future. As for who sent the tapes, I don't think there is a definitive answer. It's purposely left ambiguous. 
  - It's definitely one of the possible interpretation. I don't think Haneke himself has a single correct answer to that. He definitely wanted to make us spectators engage with his film, and communicate to us different ideas. 

Could it have been Pierrot? Could it have been Majid? Could it have been someone we haven't seen? Could it have been Haneke himself? I think if Haneke wanted only one of these to be the answer, he would have told us explicitely. If he left so many possibilities open, perhaps he was more interested in having us consider them, than to give us a definite answer.
  - A huge mystery is what was actually said in that scene. I believe Haneke has confirmed dialogue was written, but presumably nobody but himself and the actors know it.
2. This is the interpretation of Caché I favour, although 'Haneke is sending the tapes' is a little more specific that I would go. It could be Haneke, it could be us (the audience), it could be God. But I think you miss the biggest clue in the film that supports this idea. It comes in the penultimate scene (I think?), when we see Georges return to his home for the last time. 

The scene begins with the familiar surveillance shot, the same that has been recorded onto the tapes throughout (this is the final time we see the shot). This lasts a while, as usual. But then Georges enters the shot and approaches his front door. This is new - the surveillance shot has never captured any of the characters before. He opens the door, we cut to an interior shot, and we see George enter the house from the inside. 

The first time I saw the film this edit sent a little shockwave through me. Why? The surveillance shot has, for the first time, been edited into the action of the film. A reality the film has spent 90 minutes establishing has suddenly been disrupted. 

To me, this establishes beyond doubt that there has been no boundary between the footage on the tapes and the footage we have been watching thourhgout the entire film. In effect, we could say the character of Georges has been sent tapes of an establishing shot from the film he is in. It's that edit that does it.
3. This is a great analysis. I've watched Cashé twice and each time I limited myself to thinking that, like a classic murder mystery, the only candidates for "who did it" *were the ones that we were shown physically in the film.* And it just seemed to me like the young Algerian boy had to be the most likely person unless the father of the family was creating this drama through some fit of madness.

But then I've heard theories like your's, and I have almost become convinced that there's something else not quite supernatural, but extra diegetic going on.

Have you ever seen *The Secret Cinema*? It's a black-and-white short film directed by Paul Bartel (1968). A young woman increasingly has these paranoid feelings that people are laughing at her or discussing her. The title may give away the plot, but I find it very adjacent to *Caché*. I feel Haneke must know about it!



## 2. Thoughts on Duck You Sucker

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Duck You Sucker aka Once Upon a Time in the Revolution aka Fistful of Dynamite is in my opinion Sergio Leone's most underrated and overlooked film. I think there are a few major reasons for this. First of all, that title doesn't really work, I think it made sense to Leone but does not seem to translate well to English as well as the fact that it is known by 3 different titles maybe turned people off or confused them. Also, even though I have been a fan of the film for a long time I only recently watched a good quality, high-def version (it's free on youtube) and it made a really big difference even though I already loved the film. It's a shame this great film from a master director has been so hard to find. 

Last reason it may not been as known or acclaimed is admittedly the film does take a long time to get going. The first 45 minutes basically only accomplishes introducing the characters and a vague sense of where they are headed. However, I think the second half of the film may be some of Leone's best work as a director. Duck You Sucker has the epic battles of The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly, the intricate plot and flashbacks like Once Upon a Time in the West and the moral complexity of Once Upon a Time in America. The film becomes more epic but also more serious and elegiac has it goes on. It gets a lot darker than Leone's other work while also developing complexity/irony in the characters. 

The movie depicts the Mexican Revolution and revolutions in general without trying to force a message about them being right or wrong. Instead it shows revolution in a complex way and lets the viewer come to their conclusion. To me, Rod Steiger and James Coburn seemed miscast at first but as the film continues and their characters become deeper and more haunted they both do a great job with excellent chemistry. Juan becoming a hero of the Revolution is a great ironic outcome and John's past and changing view of the revolution make him just as intriguing. Of course, being a Sergio Leone film, the cinematography, camera movement and epic scenes of violence are also mind-blowingly good, not to mention some of the most badass explosions in film. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Morricone score is my favorite of his. The March of the Beggars is on my playlist (dig the burping). The sequence is also hilarious.. I think you nailed it: the first half drags and isn’t particularly funny, but the second is a grand adventure and very sad. Absolutely underrated 

## 3. Susannah York going insane at the moors in Robert Altman's "IMAGES", a film which came and went.

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I didn't know how to take IMAGES. I decided to see it because it was oddly reminiscent of REPULSION though IMAGES is more surreal and abstract as a picture. I was often confused about what was exactly happening. 

I liked the metaphors, the ambiguity, the fact something shocking or surprising kept happening and Susannah York played the most subtle version of madness. She's not shrieking or chewing the scenery, it's almost as if she's like Alice in Wonderland, she's discovering secrets or facets as the plot continues. 

  
The ending, I was lost for words. The ending is brilliant and shocking. Even if you didn't like the film, the ending is worth the wait. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. To me this film feels much closer to some of Bergman’s 1960s work, the psychological horror films and chamber dramas like “The Passion of Anna,” “Hour of the Wolf” and “Persona.” Just two people in an isolated area slowly losing their sense of identity and reality together. The cinematography is top notch, coming during Vilmos Zsigmond’s peak of creativity. Not one of Altman’s greatest films, but solidly one of his best looking minor works.

## 4. [SPOILERS] The Bride question about the framing device

**Score:** 0
**Body:** A lot of people seem stuck on the Mary Shelley “framing device” and end up calling it a plot hole. She is the spine of the story!

1. In the original Frankenstein, the Bride never lives In Mary Shelley’s 1818 novel, the female creature is never actually born. Victor starts to make her, panics about what two creatures might do together, and literally tears her to pieces before animating her. So, she never breathes, she never speaks and she never gets a name or a perspective.She exists as a possibility and then as dismembered remains. The “Bride of Frankenstein” we all picture is not from the novel, she’s a later invention.

2. Whale filled that void one way, Gyllenhaal fills it another. James Whale’s 1935 Bride of Frankenstein solves this absence by: Giving us a camp, iconic Bride with the famous hair and hiss and Framing the film with a fictionalized ditzy Mary at a house party, spinning “one more story” to entertain her guests. That Mary is already an invented device (a playful “author” figure justifying why we now have a Bride at all). Maggie Gyllenhaal escalates it. Instead of “I made this up at a party,” she asks: what if the woman who wrote Frankenstein was haunted by the story she couldn’t tell, and by the woman she never got to write? So Mary doesn’t just invades yhe story.

3. Why possession, and why Ida? The film opens with Mary, in black Victorian dress, saying plainly that she has a story festering in her brain and that she’s pushing the tumor aside to tell that story. Then she possesses Ida, a woman who: 

Has almost no voice in her own life (her first “I’d prefer not to” gets an oyster shoved down her throat).

Has no solid sense of self, no “spiritual boundaries,” which makes her easy to inhabit. 

There’s also a clear parallel with Mary Shelley herself. Frankenstein was first published anonymously in 1818 then Mary’s name only appeared later on the 1831 edition, in a male literary marketplace that constrained what women could publish. The female creature that could have been a whole other story is violently erased before she exists.  Mary possessing Ida is a Gothic way of saying: The unwritten woman in Frankenstein didn’t just disappear. She became a wound in the author and in the culture.Ida’s emptiness mirrors the textual void where the Bride should have been.

There’s even a spiritual parallel. In older spiritual lore, the concept of being claimed or chosen by a spirit is sometimes described in bridal terms like you belong to the spirit, you are their vessel, their bride, their chosen one.That’s what happens when Mary claims Ida. It’s the joining of two incomplete selves. Mary gives Ida voice, fury, and direction, power that comes at the cost of her body’s autonomy. Like The Chilling Adventures of Sabrina, it’s the same scary logic of being the “bride” of a spirit or dark force that promises power but demands surrender. Yet Ida does something radical by using that power to turn against her possessor. The spirit that entered her to speak ends up teaching her how to speak for herself.

4. Good intention does not cancel violation Mary forcing herself into Ida's body is what sets the whole fight in motion. Ida starts as a woman with no voice and no spiritual boundaries. The possession forces her to fight back against the world around her and  against the spirit inhabiting her. L If you track how often we see or hear Mary, it lines up almost perfectly with Ida’s development. Early on, Ida is barely there as a person. Mary is loud, insistent, steering the narrative through her. As Ida gains a voice,saving Frank, refusing proposals, choosing violence when she needs to; Mary appears less and less. In the end, when Ida screams for Mary, Mary doesn’t come. Ida is finally alone in her own body, making a choice that belongs only to her.

5. Mary inside her own story is the gothic logic of the film. Over the past few weeks I’ve seen a lot of “Is this supposed to be real events, or Mary’s invention, or some secret sequel to the novel?” as if the film owes us a single, tidy lane. The whole Gothic mode thrives on m dream and reality leaking into each other, author and creation trading places, ghosts crossing the line into flesh. 

Mary stepping into the world of her own fiction is a literary device. She’s so consumed by the story she couldn’t write that she starts to live alongside it, bleeding into it. You can literally see that on Ida’s body: the black crystal pallid fluid used in the experiment stained the bride’s skin and looks like splashes of ink, as if Mary is writing through her skin. The film treats the Bride’s body as both text and character at once, which is about as Gothic as it gets.

If you cut Mary out, Ida is just:

A sex worker killed and reanimated.

A chaotic, angry woman on a crime spree.

A figure the world chases and executes.

With Mary in, the same events become:

The unwritten Bride from the original novel finally forcing her way into existence.

The author’s ghost pushing too far, and the “character” pushing back until she can stand alone.

Two women separated by centuries but sharing the same wound. Mary recognises that emptiness because she lived it. She doesn’t choose Ida despite her silence; she chooses her because of it

FYI: 

These are just my interpretations, shaped by my background and experience; I’m not claiming to know the director’s intent. I’m especially interested in comments that respond to the points I raise here rather than just whether the movie is good or bad overall.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Didn't you already post this exact post on this subreddit a few days ago, as well as posting this exact post on several other subreddit a few days ago? I know I saw it several times before including here, and there was some discussion in the comments. Why are you reposting it again? 
  - Yeah, it’s the same. I originally posted it in another sub but Reddit wouldn’t let me cross‑post it here, so I had to repost the text. I figured r/TrueFilm might have a different angle on it and I was interested in that discussion too. If it breaks a rule I’m happy for the mods to remove it, but I wasn’t trying to spam.
  - Yeah, I remember the same. It’s weird. I also think it’s investing way too much into something that (as I understand) was added/expanded during reshoots.
    - Even if the Mary Shelley material was expanded in reshoots, Maggie Gyllenhaal has spoken about it publicly and said she added it after early screenings because she wanted it in, it wasn’t a studio imposition, it was her own creative decision she pushed to include. And honestly, ‘it was added in reshoots’ doesn’t make a thematic reading invalid anyway. 

The fact that it was deepened after feedback suggests it resonated, not that it was slapped on randomly. Also, I’m not sure how ‘it was added in reshoots’ tells you anything about whether the analysis is correct or not.

I couldn’t cross post it on here, that is why I am reposting the text. I was also interested in a different angle/discussion on here. 

---
